elements.sent-2411-001: As text-to-image models grow increasingly powerful and complex, their burgeoning size presents a significant obstacle to widespread adoption, especially on resource-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper presents a pioneering study on post-training pruning of Stable Diffusion 2, addressing the critical need for model compression in text-to-image domain.
elements.sent-2411-003: Unlike previous work focused on language models or traditional image generation, our study tackles the complex multimodality generation models, and particularly examines the pruning impact on the textual component and the image generation component separately.
elements.sent-2411-004: We conduct a comprehensive comparison on pruning the model or the single component of the model in various sparsities.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results yield previously undocumented findings.
elements.sent-2411-006: For example, contrary to established trends in language model pruning, we discover that simple magnitude pruning outperforms more advanced techniques in text-to-image context.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, our results show that Stable Diffusion 2 can be pruned to 38.5\% sparsity with minimal quality loss, achieving a significant reduction in model size.
elements.sent-2411-008: We propose an optimal pruning configuration that prunes the text encoder to 47.5\% and the diffusion generator to 35\%.
elements.sent-2411-009: This configuration maintains image generation quality while substantially reducing computational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-010: In addition, our work uncovers intriguing questions about information encoding in text-to-image models: we observe that pruning beyond certain thresholds leads to sudden performance drops (unreadable images), suggesting that specific weights encode critical semantics information.
elements.sent-2411-011: This finding opens new avenues for future research in model compression, interoperability, and bias identification in text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-012: By providing crucial insights into the pruning behavior of text-to-image models, our study lays the groundwork for developing more efficient and accessible AI-driven image generation systems.\footnote{We publicly released our code at: https://github.com/samarthramesh/SD2-Pruning.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Large language models (LLMs) and diffusion models often face the challenge of having exceptionally large model sizes~\cite{ko2023large, brown2020language .}
elements.sent-2411-014: While these extensive parameters allow them to understand high-quality text and generate images, they also result in substantial computational demands and resource consumption~\cite{effficient_large-scale, brown2020language, diffusion_model_comprehensive_survey .}
elements.sent-2411-015: With billions of parameters, these models become prohibitively expensive to operate, limiting their use primarily to large corporations.
elements.sent-2411-016: To address this issue, significant research has been devoted to model compression techniques for deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-017: Compressing these models can make them deployable on edge devices~\cite{prakash2022iot, quantization_deployment_microcontrolers, dnn_deployment , reduce response times~\cite{hawks2021ps, liang2021pruning , enable real-time applications~\cite{niu2020patdnn, quantization_deployment_microcontrolers , and greatly increase their accessibility.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Quantization~\cite{gray_quantization_1998 and pruning~\cite{lecun_optimal_1989, hassibi1993optimal, han_learning_2015 are two primary compression approaches.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Quantization focuses on reducing the precision of a model's weights, while pruning seeks to identify and eliminate redundant weights.
elements.sent-2411-020: Both methods have been well studied for LLMs but much less has been done for vision language models~\citep{LOPES2024104015 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Further, recent advancements have introduced post-training pruning methods for LLMs, which offer compressed models without the need for retraining~\cite{frantar_sparsegpt_2023, sun_simple_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, there has been relatively little research on post-training pruning for vision language models.
elements.sent-2411-023: As far as we know, no prior work has addressed post-training pruning of text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-024: This paper seeks to fill this gap by investigating the impact of post-training pruning on Stable Diffusion 2~\citep{rombach_high-resolution_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, this study examines how pruning impacts the textual encoder and the image generator of the model separately, and identifies the optimal sparsity levels for each component.
elements.sent-2411-026: The field of image generation was initially dominated by Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)~\cite{goodfellow_generative_2014, brock2018large and Diffusion models~\cite{sohl-dickstein_deep_2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: While GANs produce high-quality images, their variability is constrained by data used in the adversarial training process and they require substantial effort to scale for diverse, complex applications~\cite{karras2019style .}
elements.sent-2411-028: On the other hand, Diffusion models, built on denoising autoencoders, excel across various image synthesis tasks~\cite{ho_denoising_2020, song2020score , but their high computational demands, requiring extensive GPU resources for training and inference, present significant challenges~\cite{dhariwal2021diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The Stable Diffusion models released by Stability AI have made significant advances in text-to-image generation and are recognized as state-of-the-art~\cite{rombach2021highresolution, esser_scaling_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: These models build upon diffusion models by incorporating a variational autoencoder to encode images in a latent space, offering several advantages over training in the image space, such as significantly reducing the computational resources needed for both training and inference~\cite{rombach2021highresolution .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The diffusion process is applied in this latent space, with cross-attention layers used to condition the model on rich representations of the input.
elements.sent-2411-032: For text-to-image tasks, this representation is generated by a transformer model~\cite{rombach_high-resolution_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this study, we focus on Stable Diffusion 2, which uses the CLIP model's text encoder~\cite{radford_learning_2021 as the transformer and a U-Net~\cite{ronneberger2015u as the diffusion model.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Stable Diffusion 2, a state-of-the-art model and a foundational architecture for later versions~\cite{esser_scaling_2024, ramesh2022hierarchical , contains 1.2 billion parameters, with 340 million in the CLIP text encoder and 860 million in the U-Net diffusion generator.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Its large size makes it an ideal candidate for model compression techniques.
elements.sent-2411-036: Pruning works on the principle that some connections in a network are of less importance than others~\cite{lecun_optimal_1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The method of finding these connections is of utmost importance for the post-training pruning.
elements.sent-2411-038: We introduce state-of-the-art pruning methods experimented in LLMs and their main difference is how they identify the weights to be pruned.
elements.sent-2411-039: Magnitude-based pruning~\cite{han_learning_2015 is one of the simplest and most commonly used techniques for pruning neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-040: It operates under the assumption that a connection's importance correlates with the absolute value of its weight.
elements.sent-2411-041: As described in Equation \ref{magnitude_pruning , the weights are ranked according to their magnitude, and the least important weights are pruned by setting them to zero.}
elements.sent-2411-042: While this method may not provide optimal performance compared to more advanced techniques, it is easy to implement, computationally efficient, and serves as a strong baseline for further refinement. \begin{equation}\label{magnitude_pruning} \text{Low Importance Connections} = \operatorname*{argmin}_i |w_i|\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-043: SparseGPT~\cite{frantar_sparsegpt_2023 tackles the task by attempting to solve the problem of identifying the optimal pruning mask.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The goal is to find a sparse subset of weights that minimizes reconstruction error.
elements.sent-2411-045: While the exact solution to this problem requires $O(d^4)$ time complexity~\cite{frantar_sparsegpt_2023 , where $d$ is the dimension of the hidden layer, SparseGPT achieves an approximate solution in $O(d^3)$, offering a more efficient approach.}
elements.sent-2411-046: This method has consistently outperformed magnitude pruning across LLMs of various sizes.
elements.sent-2411-047: Weight and Activation Pruning (Wanda)~\cite{sun_simple_2023 is a more recent method that surpasses SparseGPT in performance and has become widely adopted in various text-based models.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Wanda is grounded in the observation that emergent properties in LLMs are often associated with the presence of ``outliers''~\cite{dettmers2022gpt3 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: These outliers are defined as exceptionally large output values from neurons, which can be up to 100 times greater than typical output values.
elements.sent-2411-050: Such activations serve as valuable indicators of the importance of specific weights.
elements.sent-2411-051: Wanda extends the magnitude pruning by incorporating the values of the activations that serve as inputs for these weights.
elements.sent-2411-052: The scoring metric for this method is expressed in Equation \ref{wanda_scoring , where \(W_{ij}\) represents the value of the weights, \(X_j\) denotes the activations input to \(W_{ij}\), and \(\|X_j\|_2\) is the \(l_2\) norm of these activations.}
elements.sent-2411-053: This approach allows for a more nuanced assessment of weight importance, contributing to improved pruning effectiveness. \begin{equation}\label{wanda_scoring} S_ij = |W_{ij}| . ||X_j||_2\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: A significant observation regarding outliers in LLMs is that the density of observed outliers within a particular layer correlates with that layer's importance to the model's emergent properties~\cite{kovaleva2021bert, puccetti2022outliers, timkey2021all .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Based on this insight, Outlier-Weighted Layerwise Pruning (OWL)~\cite{yin_outlier_2024 seeks to distribute sparsity across different layers of the model in an uneven manner.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The required layer-wise sparsity is influenced by the number of outliers present; consequently, layers deemed more important are pruned less aggressively, while those considered less critical are pruned more extensively.
elements.sent-2411-057: OWL can be integrated with other pruning algorithms, such as Magnitude and Wanda as it does not specify which weights to prune; rather, it provides recommendations for local sparsity levels within the layers.
elements.sent-2411-058: While post-training pruning methods have been extensively studied for language models, their effectiveness on text-to-image models remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given the distinct architectures and training protocols of text-to-image models, it is challenging to infer how these pruning techniques, originally developed for language models, will perform in this different context.
elements.sent-2411-060: This paper investigates the pruning of Stable Diffusion 2, a widely used text-to-image generation model.
elements.sent-2411-061: Stable Diffusion 2 has 1.2 billion parameters of which 340 million parameters (28\%) are in the CLIP text encoder and 860 million parameters (72\%) are in the U-Net diffusion generator.
elements.sent-2411-062: Our initial experiments focus on examining the effects of independently pruning individual components of the model while maintaining the integrity of the other parts.
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, only prune the text encoder component and keep the diffusion generator untouched.
elements.sent-2411-064: Following this, full model pruning is explored across multiple axes to determine the optimal balance of sparsities between the text encoder and the diffusion generator.
elements.sent-2411-065: The evaluation of the best configuration for full model pruning is informed by the results obtained from the individual component pruning experiments.
elements.sent-2411-066: For text-encoder-only pruning, four different techniques are tested, namely magnitude pruning, Wanda, magnitude pruning with OWL and Wanda with OWL.
elements.sent-2411-067: For each of these techniques, multiple sparsities are tested in steps of 10\% and more granular tests are performed at intervals of interest.
elements.sent-2411-068: To date, there has been limited research on post-training pruning specifically for diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-069: Some studies have identified efficient pruning techniques that involve retraining~\cite{fang_structural_2023 , and a recent proposal introduced a novel approach for iteratively pruning Latent Diffusion models~\cite{castells_ld-pruner_2024 , such as Stable Diffusion.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, the scope of this paper is limited to magnitude pruning for the diffusion component of the model, leaving the exploration of other techniques for future research.
elements.sent-2411-071: As with the text encoder, multiple sparsity levels are evaluated in increments of 10\%, and more detailed assessments are conducted at intervals of particular interest.
elements.sent-2411-072: Two distinct approaches are employed for the experiments to find the optimal configuration for full model pruning.
elements.sent-2411-073: The first approach aims to determine the best ratio of sparsities between the text encoder and image diffusion generator if a specific full model sparsity is desired.
elements.sent-2411-074: At various levels of full model sparsity, the distribution of this sparsity between the two components is outlined in Table \ref{tab:full_model_sparsities_table .}
elements.sent-2411-075: It is important to note that certain ratios associated with higher full model sparsities are unfeasible due to the significantly smaller size of the text encoder compared to the diffusion generator.
elements.sent-2411-076: For example, when aiming to prune the full model to 50\%, attempting to distribute the sparsity in a 75:25 ratio for text and image would result in pruning the text encoder to 177\%, which is clearly not achievable.
elements.figure-2411-001: 0.85{ \begin{tabular}{cccc} Full Model Sparsity Text:Image Ratio Text Sparsity Image Sparsity 20\% 75:25 53\% 7\% 20\% 50:50 35\% 14\% 20\% 25:75 18\% 21\% 30\% 75:25 80\% 10\% 30\% 50:50 53\% 21\% 30\% 25:75 27\% 31\% 40\% 50:50 71\% 28\% 40\% 25:75 35\% 42\% 50\% 50:50 89\% 35\% 50\% 25:75 44\% 52\% 60\% 25:75 53\% 63\% \end{tabular} } \caption{Sparsity is distributed between components in the ratios shown in Text:Image Ratio and Text and Image Sparsities represent the fraction of weights pruned in those components} \label{tab:full_model_sparsities_table}
elements.sent-2411-077: The second approach to full model pruning relies on the findings from the individual component pruning experiments.
elements.sent-2411-078: Both the text encoder and the diffusion generator exhibit identifiable drop-off points in performance, which will be further examined in the results section.
elements.sent-2411-079: Assuming that the sub-models remain largely unaffected until reaching these drop-off points, the full model is pruned to align with the drop-off thresholds of both sub-models.
elements.sent-2411-080: This leads to a recommended configuration for maximal pruning that balances performance and sparsity effectively.
elements.sent-2411-081: We use the Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context (MSCOCO) 2017 dataset \cite{lin_microsoft_2015 which has a large number of real images and corresponding captions.}
elements.sent-2411-082: MSCOCO is a commonly used dataset for computer vision tasks.
elements.sent-2411-083: For our experiments, we use 10,000 randomly sampled images and their corresponding captions.
elements.sent-2411-084: The Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) \cite{heusel_gans_2018 defines a distance metric between two sets of images and measures how different the two sets are.}
elements.sent-2411-085: A lower FID indicates that the two sets of images are very similar.
elements.sent-2411-086: In our experiments, we use the FID to compare images generated from MSCOCO captions with corresponding real images.
elements.sent-2411-087: For each model we generate 10,000 images to calculate the FID.
elements.sent-2411-088: The CLIP Score \cite{hessel_clipscore_2022 uses a multi-modal text and image model to directly compare a generated image with the provided prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-089: It uses the CLIP model to calculate the similarity and a lower CLIP Score indicates a higher correlation in the semantic content of the prompt and image.
elements.sent-2411-090: In our experiments, for each pruned model, we generate 10,000 images and calculate the average similarity of each image with its prompt using the CLIP Score.
elements.sent-2411-091: The primary objective of this study is to analyze the impact of pruning on text-to-image models, specifically focusing on the individual components of Stable Diffusion 2, namely the CLIP text encoder and the U-Net diffusion generator.
elements.sent-2411-092: Our aim is to explore the optimal trade-off between model performance and computational resource efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-093: We initially approach this problem by examining the effects of pruning when targeting only one component at a time.
elements.sent-2411-094: The insights gained from this analysis subsequently inform our strategy for full model pruning.
elements.sent-2411-095: All experimentation is quantitatively evaluated using FID~\cite{heusel_gans_2018 and CLIP Score~\cite{hessel_clipscore_2022 metrics on 10,000 images generated from captions present in the MSCOCO 2017 dataset~\cite{lin_microsoft_2015 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: These metrics are reinforced by qualitative evaluation of generated image quality.
elements.sent-2411-097: We experiment with two pruning methods on the CLIP text encoder and extend these by applying OWL to test whether outlier weighting improves performance in text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-098: Figures~\ref{fig:sd2_text_magnitude_only_plots , \ref{fig:sd2_text_wanda_only_plots , \ref{fig:sd2_text_owl_magnitude_only_plots , \ref{fig:sd2_text_owl_wanda_only_plots show the FID and CLIP scores for each method.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: At lower sparsity levels, all techniques result in minimal performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-100: However, as sparsity increases, each method experiences a sharp performance decline at specific thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-101: Qualitative evaluations of the generated images corroborate these quantitative results.
elements.sent-2411-102: As the simplest and most straightforward method, magnitude pruning serves as an important baseline.
elements.sent-2411-103: When pruning up to a sparsity of 60\%, the model performs similarly to the original, with only a minimal decline in performance.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, at 62.5\% sparsity, the model experiences a sudden drop in performance.
elements.sent-2411-105: This is further confirmed by visual inspection of the generated images, which show a clear degradation in quality.
elements.sent-2411-106: At 62.5\%, the model struggles to correctly interpret the prompt, as evidenced by a misframed dog and the absence of a field in the images.
elements.sent-2411-107: Beyond this threshold, the model's performance deteriorates drastically, with the generated images becoming complete noise.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/sd2_text_magnitude_only_plots.png} \caption{Pruning only Text Encoder of Stable Diffusion 2 using Magnitude Pruning - Sharp drop off in performance observed at 62.5\%} \label{fig:sd2_text_magnitude_only_plots}
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfloat[0\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/magnitude_only-full.png} } \subfloat[50\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/magnitude_only-0.5.png} } \subfloat[62.5\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/magnitude_only-0.625.png} } \subfloat[65\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/magnitude_only-0.65.png} } \caption{Text Encoder Magnitude Pruning Examples - Text Encoder breaks down beyond 62.5\%} \label{fig:magnitude_only_pruning_examples}
elements.sent-2411-108: Despite being one of the most effective pruning techniques for many language models, Wanda appears to be poorly suited for pruning the text component in this text-to-image model.
elements.sent-2411-109: When compared to the baseline magnitude pruning, Wanda exhibits similar behavior but consistently underperforms.
elements.sent-2411-110: Like magnitude pruning, Wanda shows minimal impact on performance up to a certain threshold, after which there is a sharp decline.
elements.sent-2411-111: However, for Wanda, this threshold occurs at 60\% sparsity, making it less effective than magnitude pruning.
elements.sent-2411-112: An interesting observation arises at 80\% sparsity, where the FID score is unexpectedly lower than at 70\%, suggesting that Wanda might perform better at higher sparsities.
elements.sent-2411-114: Additionally, when visually inspecting the images in Figure \ref{fig:wanda_only_pruning_examples , the deterioration threshold appears to be around 62.5\%, slightly different from the 60\% threshold indicated by the FID and CLIP Score metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-115: This discrepancy is likely due to the specific behavior of the model on this particular prompt, whereas the metrics reflect the average performance across 10,000 distinct prompts.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/sd2_text_wanda_only_plots.png} \caption{Pruning only Text Encoder of Stable Diffusion 2 using Wanda Pruning - Sharp drop off in performance observed at 60\%} \label{fig:sd2_text_wanda_only_plots}
elements.figure-2411-005: \subfloat[0\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/wanda_only-full.png} } \subfloat[50\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/wanda_only-0.5.png} } \subfloat[62.5\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/wanda_only-0.625.png} } \subfloat[65\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/wanda_only-0.65.png} } \caption{Text Encoder Wanda Pruning Examples - Text Encoder breaks down beyond 62.5\%} \label{fig:wanda_only_pruning_examples}
elements.sent-2411-116: Applying outlier weighting to magnitude pruning shows minimal impact overall.
elements.sent-2411-117: While some sparsities exhibit slight improvements, the differences are generally insignificant.
elements.sent-2411-118: This finding aligns with the observations made by the authors of the OWL paper, who noted that outlier weighting tends to offer meaningful improvements primarily at higher sparsity levels.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/sd2_text_owl_magnitude_only_plots.png} \caption{Pruning only Text Encoder of Stable Diffusion 2 using Magnitude Pruning with OWL - Sharp drop off in performance observed at 62.5\%} \label{fig:sd2_text_owl_magnitude_only_plots}
elements.figure-2411-007: \subfloat[0\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_magnitude_only-full.png} } \subfloat[50\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_magnitude_only-0.5.png} } \subfloat[62.5\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_magnitude_only-0.625.png} } \subfloat[65\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_magnitude_only-0.65.png} } \caption{Text Encoder Magnitude Pruning with OWL Examples - Text Encoder breaks down beyond 62.5\%} \label{fig:owl_magnitude_only_pruning_examples}
elements.sent-2411-119: As with magnitude pruning, applying outlier-based weighting to Wanda pruning does not result in significant improvements.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/sd2_text_owl_wanda_only_plots.png} \caption{Pruning only Text Encoder of Stable Diffusion 2 using Wanda Pruning with OWL - Sharp drop off in performance observed at 60\%} \label{fig:sd2_text_owl_wanda_only_plots}
elements.figure-2411-009: \subfloat[0\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_wanda_only-full.png} } \subfloat[50\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_wanda_only-0.5.png} } \subfloat[62.5\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_wanda_only-0.625.png} } \subfloat[65\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/owl_wanda_only-0.65.png} } \caption{Text Encoder Wanda Pruning with OWL Examples - Text Encoder breaks down beyond 60\%} \label{fig:owl_wanda_only_pruning_examples}
elements.sent-2411-120: The finding that Wanda pruning is outperformed by magnitude pruning is quite surprising and underscores the need for pruning techniques tailored specifically to text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-121: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:pruning_comparions_line_plot , magnitude pruning consistently outperforms Wanda at every sparsity level.}
elements.sent-2411-122: This is particularly notable given that, in most NLP models, magnitude pruning is typically the weakest among post-training pruning methods.
elements.sent-2411-123: This suggests a significant opportunity to develop specialized pruning algorithms for text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-124: Another key observation is that Outlier Weighted Layerwise (OWL) pruning, when applied on top of both magnitude and Wanda pruning, results in only marginal improvements.
elements.sent-2411-125: Figure \ref{fig:pruning_comparions_line_plot also highlights the sharp performance drop-off, a pattern observed across all pruning algorithms tested.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/pruning_comparions_line_plot.png} \caption{Comparing Pruning Techniques for pruning only text encoder} \label{fig:pruning_comparions_line_plot}
elements.sent-2411-126: For the diffusion component of the model, we observe a more gradual decline in performance as pruning increased.
elements.sent-2411-127: The model maintains strong performance at lower sparsity levels, but its performance steadily decreases as sparsity exceeded 40\%.
elements.sent-2411-128: Based on a qualitative analysis, 50\% sparsity is identified as the point where the model's performance significantly deteriorated, and this threshold is later used in full model pruning.
elements.sent-2411-129: This gradual decline is evident when examining the pruned images from 30\% to 60\%, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:diffusion_magnitude_only_gradual_shift in the appendix.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/sd2_image_magnitude_only_plots.png} \caption{Pruning only Image Diffusion Generator of Stable Diffusion 2 using Magnitude Pruning - Gradual decline in performance} \label{fig:sd2_image_magnitude_only_plots}
elements.figure-2411-012: \subfloat[0\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/diffusion_only-full.png} } \subfloat[40\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/diffusion_only-0.4.png} } \subfloat[50\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/diffusion_only-0.5.png} } \subfloat[60\%]{ \includegraphics[width=0.225\textwidth]{images/pruning_examples/diffusion_only-0.6.png} } \caption{Diffusion Generator Magnitude Pruning Examples - Performance greatly suffers beyond 40\%} \label{fig:diffusion_magnitude_only_pruning_examples}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Comparison between Text and Image Pruning
elements.sent-2411-130: To address the question of which component—the text encoder or the image diffusion generator—yields better results when pruned, we compare the best-performing pruned models for each approach.
elements.sent-2411-131: This comparison is presented in Table \ref{tab:text_image_comparison .}
elements.figure-2411-013: 0.9{ \begin{tabular}{cccccc} Total Sparsity Text Sparsity Image Sparsity FID CLIP Score 16.9\% 60\% 0\% \cellcolor{green!25}21.7 0.282 28.7\% 0\% 40\% 25.38 \cellcolor{green!25}0.311 \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison between best model obtained pruning each component individually} \label{tab:text_image_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-132: Comparing the two models, which qualitatively produce similar results, reveals some intriguing findings.
elements.sent-2411-133: First, pruning the image diffusion generator achieves greater overall sparsity due to its larger size, contributing more significantly to the full model’s sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-134: Additionally, pruning the text encoder results in a worse CLIP score, while pruning the image diffusion generator leads to a worse FID score.
elements.sent-2411-135: This raises interesting questions about what these metrics are actually measuring.
elements.sent-2411-136: In particular, the lower CLIP score for text encoder pruning may be due to a potential bias, as the text encoder itself is a CLIP model, which could influence how its performance is evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-137: Given the computational demands of evaluating models, conducting an exhaustive grid search to identify the optimal pruning configuration is impractical.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the first approach we try to establish guiding principles for how to best distribute sparsity given a certain target sparsity for the full model.
elements.sent-2411-139: The experiments shown in Table \ref{tab:full_model_sparsities_table were conducted and the results are shown in Tables \ref{tab:full_model_fid_scores , \ref{tab:full_model_clip_scores , \ref{tab:full_model_sparsities_results .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: As outlined in the methodology section, certain configurations are deemed invalid due to the significant size disparity between the text and image components.
elements.sent-2411-141: All experiments used magnitude pruning for both components of the model.
elements.sent-2411-142: Similar experiments were conducted using magnitude pruning with OWL for the CLIP text encoder and produced very similar results as seen in Tables \ref{tab:owl_full_model_fid_scores , \ref{tab:owl_full_model_clip_scores , \ref{tab:owl_full_model_sparsities_results in the appendix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Tables \ref{tab:full_model_fid_scores , \ref{tab:full_model_clip_scores , \ref{tab:full_model_sparsities_results demonstrate that the model performs better when the majority of the sparsity is allocated to the image component.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Notably, the configuration in which 75\% of the model's sparsity is concentrated in the image diffusion generator consistently outperforms other splits.
elements.sent-2411-145: This finding aligns with the earlier observation that pruning the image component yields greater sparsity with less decline in performance compared to pruning the text component.
elements.sent-2411-146: The models where performance deteriorates significantly are highlighted in Table \ref{tab:full_model_sparsities_results and correspond to sparsities that exceed the drop-off thresholds identified in the individual component pruning experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-147: This reinforces the consistency of these pruning thresholds and informs our second approach for determining the optimal full model pruning configuration.

elements.sent-2411-148: We examine the model pruned to both thresholds established in the individual pruning experiments.
elements.sent-2411-149: Given the possibility that these thresholds might behave differently when both components are pruned simultaneously, we also prune several models to values slightly below the thresholds to determine the optimal sparsity configurations.
elements.sent-2411-150: Starting from the two thresholds, we decrease both the text and image component sparsities in increments of 2.5\%, evaluating each of these configurations.
elements.sent-2411-151: The results are summarized in Table \ref{tab:final_pruning_configs_results , where the unpruned model is provided as a baseline and highlighted in gray.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The models were pruned using magnitude pruning for both components of the model.
elements.sent-2411-153: These configurations were also tested using magnitude pruning with OWL for CLIP text encoder and produced very similar results as shown in Table \ref{tab:final_pruning_configs_results_owl_version in the appendix.}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{images/model_reccomendation_knee.png} \caption{Model Improvement as sparsity is reduced from identified drop-off thresholds} \label{fig:model_reccomendation_knee}

elements.sent-2411-154: The results presented in Table \ref{tab:final_pruning_configs_results indicate that pruning both thresholds simultaneously leads to a model performance that is significantly worse than anticipated.}
elements.sent-2411-155: While each threshold individually resulted in minimal performance loss, the combined pruning of both components appears to degrade the model’s performance substantially.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, as we gradually reduce both sparsities, we observe improvements, which, along with qualitative evaluations of the generated images, enable us to recommend an optimal pruning configuration for the model.
elements.sent-2411-157: Specifically, Stable Diffusion 2 can be pruned to 38.5\% sparsity by adjusting the text encoder to 47.5\% sparsity and the diffusion generator to 35\% sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-158: This configuration results in minimal loss of image quality while providing a substantial reduction in model size.
elements.sent-2411-159: A comparison of the generated image quality for the baseline and optimal configurations is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:base_vs_optimal_config .}
elements.figure-2411-019: \caption*{Prompt: A dog in a field} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/base__a_dog_in_a_field.png} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/owl_magnitude-0.475__magnitude-0.35__a_dog_in_a_field.png} \end{subfigure} \caption*{Prompt: A treehouse at night} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/base__a_treehouse_at_night.png} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/owl_magnitude-0.475__magnitude-0.35__a_treehouse_at_night.png} \end{subfigure} \caption*{Prompt: An ocean sunset} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/base__an_ocean_sunset.png} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/owl_magnitude-0.475__magnitude-0.35__an_ocean_sunset.png} \end{subfigure} \caption*{Prompt: The Eiffel Tower} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/base__the_eiffel_tower.png} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/final_config_comparison_images/owl_magnitude-0.475__magnitude-0.35__the_eiffel_tower.png} \end{subfigure} \caption{Base Model vs Optimally Pruned Model at 38.5\% Sparsity} \label{fig:base_vs_optimal_config}
elements.sent-2411-160: This research presents the first comprehensive study on post-training pruning of text-to-image models, specifically focusing on Stable Diffusion 2.
elements.sent-2411-161: Our findings reveal unexpected behaviors and provide crucial insights into the compression of these complex models.
elements.sent-2411-162: We demonstrate that Stable Diffusion 2 can be effectively pruned to 38.5\% sparsity with minimal quality loss, achieving a significant reduction in model size.
elements.sent-2411-163: The optimal pruning configuration, with 47.5\% sparsity in the text encoder and 35\% in the diffusion generator, maintains image generation quality while substantially reducing computational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-164: This achievement paves the way for the deployment of advanced text-to-image models on resource-constrained devices, potentially broadening accessibility to this technology.
elements.sent-2411-165: Contrary to established trends in language model pruning, we found that simple magnitude pruning outperforms more advanced techniques in this context.
elements.sent-2411-166: Additionally, we uncovered the existence of sharp performance thresholds, suggesting a distinctive information encoding mechanism in text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-167: These unexpected results highlight the unique challenges and opportunities in pruning text-to-image models, emphasizing the need for specialized approaches in this domain.
elements.sent-2411-168: Our work opens up new avenues for future research, including the development of pruning techniques tailored to text-to-image models, further investigation into the information encoding mechanisms of these models, and exploration of potential applications in bias identification and model interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-169: In conclusion, this study not only provides practical insights for the efficient deployment of text-to-image models but also lays the groundwork for a new subfield at the intersection of model compression and text-to-image generation.
elements.sent-2411-170: As these models continue to grow in complexity and capability, the insights and methods presented here will be crucial in ensuring their widespread accessibility and efficient implementation.
elements.sent-2411-171: Our results and analyses suggest several future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our findings indicate that both Wanda and SparseGPT do not effectively extend to these models, while magnitude pruning—typically regarded as the baseline method for pruning—emerges as the best-performing approach in our study.
elements.sent-2411-173: This highlights a significant opportunity for further improvement and optimization in the field.
elements.sent-2411-174: Additionally, our work could be applied to more recent models, such as Stable Diffusion 3 or larger text-to-image models, to assess the generalizability of our findings.. .
